metadata.title: Guideline on the evaluation of medicinal products for the treatment of chronic constipation (Draft)
metadata.document_type: Draft Guideline — released for public consultation, comments deadline 31 August 2014
metadata.note: This draft guideline covers chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC), opioid-induced constipation (OIC), and bowel cleansing. A finalized version exists in the same directory (guideline-evaluation-medicinal-products-treatment-chronic-constipation-including-opioid-induced-constipation-and-bowel-cleansing_en.pdf) and should be checked for superseding rules. This document replaces the concept paper EMA/CHMP/462198/2012.

rules.rule_text: A treatment claim for chronic constipation must be specified as 'chronic functional constipation' or 'chronic idiopathic constipation'; a broad claim of 'chronic constipation' is only possible if relevant studies have also been conducted in secondary constipation.
rules.source_section: Section 4. Disease classification/possible claims — Chronic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: A treatment claim for chronic constipation should therefore in the future clearly be specified as 'chronic functional constipation' or 'chronic idiopathic constipation', in order to state that secondary constipation has not been investigated for the compound. A broad claim of 'chronic constipation' is only possible, if relevant studies have also been conducted in secondary constipation.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Secondary constipation (due to underlying disease or medication) must in all circumstances be documented in separate studies in order to obtain an indication; extrapolation from a trial population in idiopathic constipation is not considered adequate.
rules.source_section: Section 4. Disease classification/possible claims — Secondary constipation including Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: Secondary constipation (due to underlying disease (e.g. endocrine or neurological) or medication) should in all circumstances be documented in separate studies in order to obtain an indication. Secondary constipation – as has been shown for opioid induced constipation – may be more difficult to treat, and extrapolation from a trial population in idiopathic constipation is not considered to be adequate.
rules.exceptions: A general claim for 'secondary constipation' requires trials in both disease models (other diseases and medication-induced constipation).

rules.rule_text: For a claim of treatment of patients with 'previously failed therapy on usual laxatives', patients with inadequate response must be defined as having confirmed insufficient response to laxative treatment with at least two drug substances from different classes (bulking agent/fibre, osmotic laxative, or stimulant laxative) by history taking; the inadequate response to at least one of the agents must also be documented during the run-in period.
rules.source_section: Section 4. Disease classification/possible claims — Previous failed therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: For a claim of treatment of patients with 'previously failed therapy on other ('usual') laxatives' will have to be substantiated. To this end, it is requested that patients with inadequate response to 'usual laxatives' included into clinical trials will have to be adequately defined as follows: A patient with inadequate response to 'usual laxatives' should confirm insufficient response to laxative treatment with at least two drug substances belonging to different classes used in the treatment of constipation (bulking agent/fibre, osmotic laxative, or stimulant laxative) by history taking. Additionally, the inadequate response to at least one of the agents should be documented during the run-in period of the trials.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Treatment claims for bowel cleansing agents must as a rule be related to the treatment of patients scheduled for colonoscopy for diagnostic purposes; any deviating indication (other diagnostic or therapeutic procedures, including surgery) requires additional justification.
rules.source_section: Section 4. Disease classification/possible claims — Bowel cleansing
rules.verbatim_quote: Treatment claims for bowel cleansing agents should therefore – as a rule – be related to the treatment of patient scheduled for colonoscopy for diagnostic purposes. Any deviating indication – relating to other diagnostic or to therapeutic procedures, including surgery, would need additional justification.
rules.exceptions: Additional justification required for surgery or other diagnostic procedures.

rules.rule_text: For patient inclusion in CIC clinical trials, all patients must fulfil the following mandatory inclusion criteria (modified Rome III): (a) at least 2 of 3 criteria: straining during ≥25% of defecations with severity <3-4 on a 5-point scale, lumpy/hard stools in ≥25% of defecations, fewer than 3 defecations per week; AND (b) all of: loose stools rarely present without laxatives, normal rectal sphincter tonus on digital rectal examination, insufficient criteria for IBS, no other identifiable reason for constipation.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Patient selection, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: a)- A patient must fulfil 2 or more of the following 3 criteria: Straining during at least 25% of defecations but severity should be less than 3 or 4 on a 5-point scale / Lumpy or hard stools in at least 25% of defecations / fewer than 3 defecations per week. b) - A patient must fulfil all of the following: Loose stools are rarely present without the use of laxatives / Normal rectal sphincter tonus on digital rectal examination / There are insufficient criteria for IBS. / No other reasons for constipation can be identified (e.g. medication).
rules.exceptions: Patients may additionally (optionally) suffer from sensation of incomplete evacuation, anorectal obstruction/blockage, or manual manoeuvres (≥25% of defecations). Patients with suspected defecation disorders must not be included.

rules.rule_text: During the run-in period of CIC trials, the number of stools must not exceed 5 in two weeks; these criteria must be fulfilled both at recruitment (by history taking) and during the run-in phase.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Patient selection, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: These criteria should be fulfilled at recruitment (by history taking), and during the run-in phase of the studies (see Chapter 6.3). During the run-in period, the number of stools should not exceed 5 in two weeks.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: All patients in CIC trials must receive similar baseline recommendations and/or training regarding nutrition, fluid intake and exercise; patients must be evaluated for grossly inadequate hydration, total absence of fibre-containing nutrients, and level of physical exercise.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Patient selection, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: However, all patients should be evaluated for grossly inadequate hydration, total missing of fibre-containing nutrients, and level of physical exercise. All patients should receive similar baseline recommendations and/or training regarding nutrition, fluid intake and exercise.
rules.exceptions: No strong recommendations on lifestyle modification beyond standardisation across arms.

rules.rule_text: Patients entering OIC clinical trials must have an intake of opioids of at least 3 months and an onset of constipation after the start of opioid therapy; stable opioid dosing must be made a requirement for a certain period prior to inclusion for non-cancer pain patients.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Patient selection, Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients with opioid-induced constipation should have an intake of opioids of at least 3 months, and an onset of constipation after the start of opioid therapy. A certain threshold for the minimal daily dose of opioids should be applied. Stable dosing of opioids should be made a requirement for a certain period prior to inclusion into trials in patients with non-cancer pain.
rules.exceptions: Cancer pain patients: stable dosing requirement can be fulfilled less strictly; a more flexible approach is acceptable. Patients with severe aggravation of pre-existing constipation symptoms after opioids are not recommended to be included.

rules.rule_text: If the main part of development programme evidence for OIC comes from trials in non-malignant pain patients, additional trials in the malignant pain population are required for a full OIC indication; cancer pain patients entering studies must have a diagnosis of confirmed neoplasm and a life expectancy of at least 3 months.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Patient selection, Cancer-pain and non-cancer pain patients
rules.verbatim_quote: If the main part of the evidence of a development programme is expected from trials in non-malignant pain patients, additional trials in the malignant pain population will therefore be required for a full indication of OIC. Of course, patients entering these studies should have a diagnosis of confirmed neoplasm and a life expectancy of at least 3 months.
rules.exceptions: If main evidence is from cancer pain patients, an additional study in non-cancer OIC patients is required including documentation of long-term safety.

rules.rule_text: Bowel cleansing studies must report the results/outcomes of the diagnostic evaluation ('underlying pathology') in patients included, in order to at least assess the safety of patients with bowel pathology compared to those without.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Bowel cleansing
rules.verbatim_quote: Because colonoscopy (and other diagnostic procedures on the bowel) have to be performed in populations that are usually screened for bowel pathology, or are even suspected of pre-existing pathology, the results/outcome of the diagnostic evaluation – and thus the 'underlying pathology' – in the patients included in such trials should in future be reported, in order to at least assess the safety of patients with bowel pathology in comparison to those without.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For new bowel cleansing purgative regimens in early development, full monitoring of cardiovascular (blood pressure, heart rate), renal function (serum/eGFR), serum chemistry (electrolytes including magnesium, phosphorus, calcium, pH, acid-base balance), and body fluid composition (stool, urine, osmolality, electrolytes, net water balance) is required.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Bowel cleansing, Pharmacodynamic evaluation
rules.verbatim_quote: The application of either a large amount of fluid to, or the production of such by the body itself in the bowels may theoretically have an influence on a variety of parameters, encompassing not only cardiovascular (blood pressure, heart beat frequency), and renal function (serum measurements, eGFR), as well as serum chemistry (electrolytes including magnesium, phosphorus and calcium, pH, and acid-base balance), and the composition of body fluids such as stool and urine (total amount/weight, osmolality, electrolyte content/composition, total net water balance). A full monitoring of the total of these functions will be necessary during the early development of new purgative regimens.
rules.exceptions: Applies to early development of new substances/combinations for bowel cleansing.

rules.rule_text: Drug-drug interaction studies must be addressed for all constipation/bowel cleansing compounds based on the mechanism of action of the compound, and potentially via in-vitro experiments to determine adequate test substances, given that influence on motility and secretion may alter absorption of other compounds.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Interactions
rules.verbatim_quote: All substances, however, whether fulfilling the above criteria or not, will be subject to a suspicion of drug interactions due to their pharmacodynamic action, in as the influence on motility and secretion may also influence absorption of other compounds... this should in future be addressed in respective drug-drug interaction studies based on the mechanism of action of the compound, and potentially on in-vitro experiments which could help to determine adequate test substances.
rules.exceptions: Systemically available compounds with identified receptor targets should follow the standard DDI guideline.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory clinical trials for chronic idiopathic constipation must be large, double-blind, and parallel group, with a minimum treatment duration of at least 3 months, followed by an additional study period of at least 4 weeks to evaluate withdrawal and/or rebound (which can be addressed with a randomised withdrawal phase).
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Confirmatory Clinical Trials, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: Large, double-blind, parallel group clinical trials should be performed. The trials should be long enough to determine if any response will be sustained, and to cover a potential late drop-out. The duration of such studies is recommended to be at least 3 months. After the 3-months treatment period, an additional study period of at least 4 weeks should be added evaluating withdrawal and/or rebound, which can be best addressed with a randomised withdrawal phase.
rules.exceptions: Other study designs/durations must be justified in terms of their ability to adequately assess long-term sustained efficacy, withdrawal, rebound, and safety.

rules.rule_text: All CIC confirmatory trials must include a run-in phase of at least 2 weeks during which any previous active treatment is withdrawn (except defined rescue medication) and full compliance with inclusion criteria is documented.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Confirmatory Clinical Trials, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: All trials should include a run-in phase of at least 2 weeks, during which any previous active treatment is withdrawn, except a defined rescue medication, and the full compliance with the inclusion criteria is documented.
rules.exceptions: When documenting non-response to usual laxatives, the active therapy with insufficient response must be maintained during the run-in phase.

rules.rule_text: The required comparator for CIC confirmatory studies is placebo; however, at least one trial with an additional active comparator should be considered, aimed at documenting non-inferiority (or superiority) to the active comparator. Simple use of an active comparator for 'assay sensitivity' only (combined with a placebo arm) is not recommended.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Confirmatory Clinical Trials, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general rule, the comparator which is required in such studies is placebo and the comparison to placebo will normally be sufficient to conclude on the overall risk-benefit ratio of a product. However, consideration should be given to conducting at least one trial with the use of an additional active comparator... it should be aimed at documenting non-inferiority to the active comparator... Simple documentation of superiority to placebo and use of the active comparator for documenting 'assay sensitivity' only, is not recommended.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The appropriate comparator for confirmatory studies in opioid-induced constipation is placebo, because currently no clear treatment standard is available and no license has been granted in the indication.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Confirmatory Clinical Trials, Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: The appropriate comparator for studies in opioid induced constipation is considered to be placebo, because currently no clear treatment standard is available, and no license has been granted in the indication.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: OIC trials in non-malignant pain patients must include a randomised treatment period of at least 4 weeks to adequately assess efficacy and safety.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Confirmatory Clinical Trials, Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: The randomised treatment period may need to be shortened. However, it is considered that usually, an at least 4-week period may be needed to adequately assess efficacy and safety.
rules.exceptions: Shorter period may be considered for malignant pain patients to facilitate recruitment.

rules.rule_text: Bowel cleansing confirmatory studies must be active-controlled (not placebo-controlled); they must be large, randomised, and controlled, demonstrating at least non-inferiority. For non-inferiority studies, the non-inferiority margin must be clinically justified (not based on difference of active bowel cleansing medication to placebo).
rules.source_section: Section 5. Clinical Study Design — Confirmatory Clinical Trials, Bowel cleansing
rules.verbatim_quote: Studies for bowel cleansing before colonoscopy are requested to be active controlled studies, because the administration of a 'placebo regimen' is not possible or can ethically not be justified... It is therefore considered necessary to conduct large, randomised, controlled studies, in which the aim should be to demonstrate at least non-inferiority of the new substance/compound/regimen. In case a non-inferiority study is conducted the choice of the non-inferiority margin should not be based on the difference of (any) active bowel cleansing medication to placebo but on a clinically acceptable difference of the endpoint used... the proposed non-inferiority margin should be clinically justified.
rules.exceptions: Placebo administration of fluids would invalidate safety and efficacy results.

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint for CIC and OIC confirmatory trials must use a CSBM (complete spontaneous bowel movement) responder analysis: a responder is defined as having at least 3 CSBMs/week AND an increase of at least 1 CSBM/week compared to baseline; the primary evaluation must be based on an overall 75% response rate related to the total study duration (in weeks), including 'sustained response' (meeting criteria for the last 4 weeks of treatment).
rules.source_section: Section 5. Endpoints — Chronic idiopathic constipation and opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of a primary endpoint based on CSBMs will be considered to be acceptable because it incorporates spontaneity (without intake of any 'rescue' medication or any other laxative, including enema or suppository) within 24 hours before the bowel movement), as well as completeness, of the bowel movement... For the primary evaluation, a responder analysis is recommended which takes into consideration a response defined as at least 3 CSBMs/week and at the same time an increase of at least 1 CSBM/week compared to the baseline period. The primary evaluation should be based on an overall 75% response rate related to the total duration of the study (in weeks), including 'sustained response' defined as fulfilling these criteria for the last 4 weeks of treatment.
rules.exceptions: For OIC cancer pain patients where recruitment is difficult, a numerical scale based on bowel movement frequency with CSBM features and consistency (BSFS 3-4) may be used instead of a responder analysis.

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint for bowel cleansing efficacy must use a validated bowel preparation scale (e.g. Boston Bowel Preparation Scale [BBPS] or Aronchick scale); the chosen scale must be justified and its respective validation exercises presented — scales without validation will not be considered acceptable.
rules.source_section: Section 5. Endpoints — Bowel cleansing
rules.verbatim_quote: Adequate – at least partial – validation work seems to be available for the Boston Bowel Preparation Scale (BBPS)... The choice of the scale to be used as a primary endpoint should in all cases be justified and discussed, and the respective validation exercises be presented. Scales without respective validation will not be considered to be acceptable.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Separate clinical trials must be conducted in children for CIC; the inclusion of all age ranges between 0 and 18 years is considered necessary; trials must be double-blind and placebo-controlled; inclusion criteria must fully reflect the Rome III/PACCT definitions.
rules.source_section: Section 6. Studies in Special patient groups — Children, Functional constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to the different underlying pathophysiology for most of the cases of childhood constipation, the conduct of separate trials in children is generally considered to be necessary. The inclusion of all age ranges between 0 and 18 years is considered to be necessary and clinical trials should usually be conducted double-blind and placebo-controlled. The inclusion criteria should fully reflect the Rome III/PACCT definitions.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The duration of efficacy trials in the paediatric population may be 8 weeks only in cases where longer-term data are already available in adults; otherwise, long-term safety open-label extension studies must be conducted for a duration of 10 months (to make up a 12-month overall duration of treatment). In young children below age 5, efficacy evaluation must be based on caregiver information.
rules.source_section: Section 6. Studies in Special patient groups — Children, Functional constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: The duration of efficacy trials in the paediatric population can be 8 weeks only in cases where longer-term data are already available in adults. About 50% of paediatric patients appear to recover within a period of 6-12 months. Therefore, to take full account of the patients not recovering and thus being in need for long-term therapy and to document long-term safety open-label extension studies should be conducted for a duration of 10 months (in order to make up for a 12 month overall duration of treatment).
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Older patients (above 65 and above 75 years) must be recruited in phase III CIC/OIC trials at rates at least representative of the natural age range distribution; at minimum, rates informative about activity and safety in those aged above 65 are necessary.
rules.source_section: Section 6. Studies in Special patient groups — Older people
rules.verbatim_quote: The recruitment of older patients – those above 65 and those above 75 – is therefore considered desirable for all studies included in this guideline at rates that are at least representative of the natural age range distribution. In phase III trials, therefore at least rates of recruitment that are informative about activity and safety in those aged above 65 will be necessary.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: CIC clinical trials must aim to recruit at least approximately 30% of their patient population from the male gender in order to be representative; gender differences must be evaluated during early development and may require a separate programme for male and female patients.
rules.source_section: Section 6. Studies in Special patient groups — Gender, Chronic idiopathic constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: In the future, trials should aim at recruiting at least about 30% of their patient population from the male gender, in order to be representative. Gender differences should also be evaluated during early development of a compound, and if differences are found, a separate programme for male and female patients (e.g. with different doses) may be necessary. An omission of either male or female patients from the development is not considered to be desirable, unless clearly justified by grossly reduced expectance of efficacy, or grossly increased potential risks.
rules.exceptions: Omission of one gender is acceptable only if clearly justified by grossly reduced expectance of efficacy or grossly increased potential risks.

rules.rule_text: For global CIC developments recruiting from multiple regions, a sufficient proportion of patients recruited in Europe must be included unless it can be demonstrated that this is not necessary; justification must be based on analysis of ethnic/geographic and cultural factors per ICH E5 and EMA/CHMP/EWP/692792/2008 and presented at time of MAA.
rules.source_section: Section 6. Studies in Special patient groups — Geographic region
rules.verbatim_quote: For 'global developments', recruiting patients from several regions of the world, the inclusion of a sufficient proportion of patients recruited in Europe is recommended unless it can be demonstrated that this is not necessary. This justification should be based on the analysis of ethnic/geographic and cultural factors according to the requirements of the respective guidance documents (ICH E 5, EMA/CHMP/EWP/692792/2008) should be presented at the time of MAA.
rules.exceptions: Transfer of data from North American to European population may be possible for CIC and bowel cleansing but is considered different for OIC.

rules.rule_text: For OIC in global developments, a relevant proportion of patients recruited in Europe (or from regions with comparable opioid prescribing practice) must be included, given that US/European populations differ significantly in opioid prescribing practice and underlying (pain) conditions.
rules.source_section: Section 6. Studies in Special patient groups — Geographic region
rules.verbatim_quote: This is considered to be different for the condition of opioid-induced constipation for patients recruited in the US... Patients appear to receive opioids for largely different indications and for milder underlying (pain) conditions than patients in Europe. Because a population affected by more severe underlying conditions may have a relevantly different safety profile, the inclusion of a relevant proportion of patients recruited in Europe – or from regions with comparable prescribing practice of opioids – is recommended in the indication OIC.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: Long-term safety data with an observation period of at least 12 months must be available in adequate numbers to assess the safety of a medicinal product for chronic idiopathic constipation and opioid-induced constipation; ICH E1 (CHMP/ICH/375/95) is considered fully applicable.
rules.source_section: Section 7. Safety — CIC and OIC
rules.verbatim_quote: The treatment of CIC and OIC will require intermittent or continuous long-term use of medication, and it is therefore necessary to have long-term safety data with an observation period of at least 12 months available in adequate numbers to accurately assess the safety of a medicinal product. The Note for Guidance on Population Exposure: The Extent of Population Exposure to assess Clinical Safety (CHMP/ICH/375/95) is considered to be fully applicable.
rules.exceptions: For bowel cleansing (usually single administration), no long-term safety studies are required.

rules.rule_text: Laxative abuse potential must be assessed based on pharmacodynamic properties and safety evaluation results during development; laxative abuse must be part of the Risk Management Plan with appropriate observational studies proposed to be conducted post-licensing, as it cannot be adequately addressed with pre-licensing safety studies.
rules.source_section: Section 7. Safety — CIC and OIC
rules.verbatim_quote: The potential of laxative abuse – one of the oldest problems in healthcare – should be assessed based on the PD properties and the results of the safety evaluations during development. However, laxative abuse can hardly be addressed with safety studies before licensing. Therefore, laxative abuse should be part of the Risk Management Plan with appropriate observational studies to be proposed to be conducted post-licensing.
rules.exceptions: None stated.
